%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Direct Form I Realization

> **Section Objective:** Learn how a continuous-time transfer function can be implemented as a Direct Form I block diagram using integrators, gains, and adders.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Normalized transfer function
- Cascade split
- Feedforward realization of W(s)
- Feedback realization of Y(s)
- Nth-order integrator count

## 1. Put the transfer function into integrator form

Direct Form I begins by dividing the numerator and denominator of a third-order transfer function by \(s^3\), so every nonconstant term becomes a power of \(1/s\). This matters because \(1/s\) is exactly the transfer function of an integrator — so the rewritten form tells us directly how many cascaded integrators are needed and where each gain attaches.

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3\) — numerator (feedforward) gains
- \(a_1, a_2, a_3\) — denominator (feedback) gains

**Use case:** Choose this form whenever a transfer function must be drawn as an implementation block diagram.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Terms like \(1/s\), \(1/s^2\), and \(1/s^3\) signal successive integrations in the block diagram.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not treat \(s\), \(s^2\), \(s^3\) as integrators. Integrator blocks correspond to **inverse** powers of \(s\).

#### Minimal Example

For the \(b_2\) term, the branch uses two integrations of \(X(s)\), then multiplies by \(b_2\).

$$H(s)=\frac{b_0+\dfrac{b_1}{s}+\dfrac{b_2}{s^2}+\dfrac{b_3}{s^3}}{1+\dfrac{a_1}{s}+\dfrac{a_2}{s^2}+\dfrac{a_3}{s^3}}$$

## 2. Split H(s) into two realizable parts

The transfer function is factored into two cascaded subsystems:

- **\(H_1(s)\)** — the numerator factor, which maps \(X(s)\) to an intermediate signal \(W(s)\)
- **\(H_2(s)\)** — the denominator factor, which maps \(W(s)\) to the final output \(Y(s)\)

For LTI systems, the cascade order of \(H_1(s)\) and \(H_2(s)\) may be swapped mathematically. However, Direct Form I is conventionally drawn as an **input-side feedforward integrator chain** (\(H_1\)) followed by an **output-side feedback integrator chain** (\(H_2\)).

**Signals:** \(X(s)\) is the input, \(W(s)\) is the intermediate signal, \(Y(s)\) is the output.

### EXAM TRIGGER

When a realization problem asks for a block diagram from a rational transfer function, identify the numerator chain and denominator chain separately before drawing.

### COMMON MISTAKE

\(W(s)\) is **not** the final output — it is the intermediate signal between the two subsystems.

$$H(s)=\underbrace{\left(b_0+\frac{b_1}{s}+\frac{b_2}{s^2}+\frac{b_3}{s^3}\right)}_{H_1(s)}\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+\dfrac{a_1}{s}+\dfrac{a_2}{s^2}+\dfrac{a_3}{s^3}}}_{H_2(s)}$$


## 3. Build W(s) from the input-side integrator chain

Eq. (4.37) is the **feedforward half** of Direct Form I. Read it branch by branch:

| Branch | Path | Integrations |
|--------|------|--------------|
| \(b_0 X(s)\) | Direct path | 0 |
| \(b_1 X(s)/s\) | One integrator | 1 |
| \(b_2 X(s)/s^2\) | Two integrators | 2 |
| \(b_3 X(s)/s^3\) | Three integrators | 3 |

All four branches are summed at an adder to form \(W(s)\).

**Use case:** This formula tells you exactly where numerator coefficients \(b_0\) through \(b_3\) are placed in the block diagram — each attaches to the correspondingly integrated version of \(X(s)\).

### EXAM TRIGGER

Numerator coefficients \(b_k\) attach to delayed or integrated versions of the **input** signal \(X(s)\).

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not place \(b\)-coefficients on the output feedback side.

#### Near-Miss

If \(b_2\) is connected to \(Y(s)/s^2\) instead of \(X(s)/s^2\), the structure is no longer the feedforward \(H_1(s)\) realization.

$$W(s)=\left(b_0+\frac{b_1}{s}+\frac{b_2}{s^2}+\frac{b_3}{s^3}\right)X(s)\tag{4.37}$$


## 4. Build Y(s) using the output-side feedback chain

Eq. (4.39) comes from rearranging the denominator relationship between \(W(s)\) and \(Y(s)\). The output \(Y(s)\) is synthesized by starting with \(W(s)\), then **subtracting** three feedback terms:

- \(a_1 Y(s)/s\) — one integration of the output, weighted by \(a_1\)
- \(a_2 Y(s)/s^2\) — two integrations of the output, weighted by \(a_2\)
- \(a_3 Y(s)/s^3\) — three integrations of the output, weighted by \(a_3\)

**Symbol definitions:** \(W(s)\) is the signal created by the numerator chain; \(Y(s)\) is the final output; \(a_1, a_2, a_3\) are denominator coefficients.

**Use case:** This formula tells you where the feedback gains belong in the block diagram.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Denominator coefficients create **feedback** terms from integrated versions of \(Y(s)\), not from \(X(s)\).

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not use plus signs for the \(a\)-branches after rearrangement. The Direct Form I diagram shows **negative** \(a\)-gain feedback contributions.

#### Reference

Before rearrangement, the same relationship is \(W(s) = \left(1 + \dfrac{a_1}{s} + \dfrac{a_2}{s^2} + \dfrac{a_3}{s^3}\right)Y(s)\), labeled Eq. (4.38) in the text.

$$Y(s)=W(s)-\left(\frac{a_1}{s}+\frac{a_2}{s^2}+\frac{a_3}{s^3}\right)Y(s)\tag{4.39}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Normalized inverse-power form:** \(H(s)=\dfrac{b_0+b_1/s+b_2/s^2+b_3/s^3}{1+a_1/s+a_2/s^2+a_3/s^3}\) — dividing by \(s^N\) converts every term into a power of \(1/s\), the integrator transfer function.
- **Cascade split:** \(H(s)=H_1(s)\,H_2(s)\) where \(H_1(s)=b_0+b_1/s+b_2/s^2+b_3/s^3\) produces intermediate signal \(W(s)\) from \(X(s)\), and \(H_2(s)\) produces \(Y(s)\) from \(W(s)\).
- **Eq. (4.37) — feedforward realization:** \(W(s)=\left(b_0+\dfrac{b_1}{s}+\dfrac{b_2}{s^2}+\dfrac{b_3}{s^3}\right)X(s)\) — numerator coefficients \(b_k\) attach to \(k\)-times-integrated versions of \(X(s)\).
- **Eq. (4.39) — feedback realization:** \(Y(s)=W(s)-\left(\dfrac{a_1}{s}+\dfrac{a_2}{s^2}+\dfrac{a_3}{s^3}\right)Y(s)\) — denominator coefficients \(a_k\) create negative feedback from \(k\)-times-integrated versions of \(Y(s)\).
- **Integrator count:** An \(N\)th-order Direct Form I realization requires \(N\) integrators on the input side and \(N\) on the output side — **\(2N\) integrators total**.

*Next, Direct Form II will reduce the number of integrators by sharing the integrator chain.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
